methodology.primary: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) — Kernel SHAP for GNN, Tree SHAP for gradient boosted models
methodology.secondary: LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) for individual counterfactual generation
methodology.aggregation: Weighted ensemble SHAP with sign-consistent averaging

examples.context: Why recommend Iso E Super as alternative to MAT-4521 (Sandalwood Oil)

explanation.confidence_statement: 91% confidence in this substitution; 95% CI [87%, 94%]. Model: similarity-gnn-v3 (calibrated ECE=0.019).

top_factors.interpretation: The molecular fingerprint of Iso E Super shares 89.3% structural features with Sandalwood Oil. This is the single strongest predictor of olfactory similarity.
Cost reduction of $7.70/kg represents significant savings while maintaining quality. The cost delta is within the acceptable threshold for luxury fine fragrance applications.
Panel of 47 trained evaluators showed d' = 0.23 discrimination between original and substitute, well below the 0.5 threshold for consumer-noticeable difference.

explanation.evidence_summary: Iso E Super (CAS 54464-57-2) is an industry-proven synthetic with a 30-year safety record under IFRA. Its woody-amber profile with subtle cedar facets fills the same olfactory space as natural sandalwood. Molecular similarity (0.893) exceeds the 0.80 threshold for substitutability. Sensory panel data confirms non-significant hedonic difference.
limitations: Sensory panel was European demographic only (n=47); may not generalize to Asian markets where sandalwood preferences differ
Iso E Super has higher substantivity; dry-down character may evolve differently over 8+ hours
Batch-to-batch variability of Sandalwood Oil (±12% santalol content) means the similarity score has an inherent range of 0.83-0.92

data_drift.interpretation: No meaningful distribution shift detected. Training data remains representative.

counterfactual.minimal_change_scenario: If budget constraints tighten further, consider mixing Iso E Super with 15% Javanol (MAT-6672) at $31.20/kg for a 42% cost reduction while improving sandalwood fidelity to 0.91 similarity.
counterfactual.expected_outcome: 42% cost savings with 0.91 similarity; slight increase in creaminess
counterfactual.trade_off: Javanol adds a milky-sandalwood facet that may not suit all masculine fragrances

examples.context: Why Rose Givco 222 is the recommended replacement for Rose Absolute

top_factors.interpretation: Rose Absolute substitutes show strong elasticity — a 58.9% price reduction yields only a 12% sensory deviation. This ratio (4.9:1 cost-to-sensory) exceeds the acceptable 3:1 threshold.
Rose Givco 222 occupies the same Floral-Rose-Damascenone quadrant in olfactory space as Rose Absolute, with 0.94 cosine similarity in the 192-dim sensory embedding space.
Rose Givco 222 is formulated to match the phenylethyl alcohol (PEA) to citronellol ratio of natural Rose Absolute at 3:2, critical for realistic rose character.

explanation.evidence_summary: Rose Givco 222 (Givaudan) was specifically designed as a cost-effective rose base for fine fragrance. It contains 17 aroma chemicals precisely balanced to replicate the PEA/citronellol/geraniol/nerol profile of Bulgarian Rosa damascena absolute. Industry adoption by 3 of the top 5 luxury houses validates its quality.
limitations: Trace compounds (β-damascenone at 0.14%, rose oxide at 0.08%) in natural absolute are absent in the synthetic base, contributing subtle but detectable complexity differences
Natural absolute offers 'storytelling' marketing value that synthetics cannot replicate for luxury positioning
Supplier dependency: only Givaudan produces this specific base; supply chain risk exists

counterfactual.minimal_change_scenario: Retain 1.5% Rose Absolute for marketing claim while replacing remaining 6.5% with Rose Givco 222. Cost: $65.20/kg. Savings: 42.2%.
counterfactual.expected_outcome: Substantial savings with full 'contains natural rose absolute' label claim intact
counterfactual.trade_off: Higher cost than full replacement; requires two inventory SKUs

examples.context: Predicted hedonic score of 78.4 for FRM-2104

top_factors.interpretation: Hedione at 18.2% is near the 'sweet spot' (15-20%) for maximizing consumer liking. Above 22% shows diminishing returns in panel data.
The ratio of damascones to ionones (0.67) falls in the optimally-preferred range of 0.55-0.75 based on 2,400 consumer evaluations.
Using 3 musks (Galaxolide, Ethylene Brassylate, Muscenone) adds complexity but creates a slight 'muddy' dry-down. Reducing to 2 musks could improve clarity by 3-5 hedonic points.

explanation.evidence_summary: The transformer model identifies Hedione as the dominant driver of consumer liking for this formula type (floral-woody). The rose accord construction is technically sound. The musk blend complexity is slightly above optimal; simplifying could push hedonic score above 80.
limitations: Consumer panel data heavily weighted toward European and North American preferences; Asian markets (35% of luxury fragrance spend) may differ
Seasonal effects not modeled: this formula was evaluated in spring panels; summer preferences for lighter profiles not captured
Brand halo effects (bottle, marketing, celebrity endorsement) contribute 30-40% of real-world liking and are not modeled

counterfactual.minimal_change_scenario: Remove Muscenone (0.4%), increase Galaxolide from 8.2% to 8.6%
counterfactual.expected_outcome: Hedonic score rises to 82.8; cleaner dry-down at cost saving of $1.20/kg
counterfactual.trade_off: Slight reduction in musk complexity; panelists who prefer 'animalic' facets may notice absence
